title: Dog Pupils Different Sizes (Anisocoria): Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your dog's pupils are different sizes, it is a high-priority medical emergency. Learn about anisocoria causes and why you need a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Unequal pupil size (anisocoria) is a high-priority veterinary emergency that can indicate serious eye or brain issues; seek immediate care and take a photo of your dog's eyes to assist with triage.

What is anisocoria and why are my dog's pupils different sizes?

Anisocoria is a medical condition where a dog's pupils are unequal in size. One pupil may be constricted (small) while the other is dilated (large). This phenomenon is not a disease in itself but rather a clinical sign that something is affecting your dog's eye, the nerves leading to the eye, or the brain.

Is it an emergency if my dog's pupils are different sizes?

  • Uneven pupils are considered a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate evaluation.
  • This symptom can be caused by serious issues, including head trauma, glaucoma (increased eye pressure), corneal ulcers, or internal inflammation (uveitis).
  • Because these conditions can lead to permanent blindness or indicate severe neurological damage, your dog must be evaluated by a veterinarian as soon as possible.

How does taking a photo of my dog's eyes help the veterinary triage team?

  • A clear photo allows the triage team to see the severity of the pupil disparity before you arrive.
  • Staff can check for other visible symptoms like redness, cloudiness, or discharge.
  • Visual information helps the medical team prioritize your pet's care and prepare for your arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Midbrain lesions can cause contralateral conscious proprioceptive deficits and hemiparesis. Ipsilateral involvement of the cranial nerve III nucleus localizes the lesion to the midbrain. If the sympathetic upper motor neurons and parasympathetic lower motor neurons are both affected in the midbrain, the pupils will be midrange size and unresponsive to light.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: Could my dog just be born with different sized pupils?
faqs.answer: While minor variations can exist, a noticeable difference in pupil size that appears suddenly is never normal and should always be treated as a medical emergency.

faqs.question: What other symptoms should I look for?
faqs.answer: Watch for squinting, pawing at the eye, redness, a change in eye color, or any signs of disorientation and stumbling, which could indicate a neurological issue.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and prevent them from rubbing their face. If you have an Elizabethan collar (cone), place it on them immediately to prevent self-injury to the eye.
